rules.title: Singular antecedent with plural pronoun
rules.description: A pronoun must agree in number with its antecedent.
rules.pattern_description: Match singular indefinite (everyone, someone, anybody, each, every, nobody, anyone, no one) followed by plural pronoun (they, them, their, themselves) in formal register.

incorrect: Everyone should bring their own lunch.
Each student must submit their work.
correct: Everyone should bring his or her own lunch.
Somebody left his or her bag.
Each student must submit his or her work.

exceptions: Singular they is widely accepted in modern English and is mandatory when referring to non-binary individuals. Flag only in very formal/academic register.
rules.fix_hint: In formal writing, use 'his or her' or restructure (pluralize the antecedent).
rules.british_variant: Singular they more widely accepted in British English.

rules.title: 'Between you and I' error
rules.description: After prepositions, use object pronouns (me, him, her, us, them), not subject pronouns.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(between|beside|except|for|from|to|with|without|about|against|among)\s+(you|him|her|them|us)\s+and\s+(I|he|she|they|we)\b/i

incorrect: Between you and I, it's wrong.
This is for you and I.
correct: Between you and me, it's wrong.
This is for you and me.

rules.fix_hint: Replace the subject pronoun with the corresponding object pronoun.

rules.title: Object pronoun as subject
rules.description: Subject position requires subject pronouns (I, he, she, we, they), not object pronouns.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(me|him|her|them|us)\s+and\s+(I|he|she|they|we)\s+(is|are|was|were|went|had|has|have|will|would|can|could|shall|should|did|do|does)\b/i

incorrect: Me and him went to the store.
correct: He and I went to the store.

exceptions: Informal speech widely uses object pronouns in compound subjects.
rules.fix_hint: Replace object pronouns with subject pronouns when used as the subject.

rules.title: Who/whom after prepositions
rules.description: After prepositions (to, for, with, by, about, from), use 'whom' not 'who'.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(to|for|with|by|about|from|at|of|between|among)\s+who\b/i

incorrect: To who did you give it?
correct: To whom did you give it?

exceptions: Informal English widely uses 'who' in all positions. Flag only in formal register.
rules.fix_hint: Replace 'who' with 'whom' after prepositions.

rules.title: Reflexive pronoun used as subject or object substitute
rules.description: Reflexive pronouns (myself, yourself, himself, etc.) should only be used when subject and object are the same person, or for emphasis.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(contact|call|email|reach|send|give|tell|ask|invite|join)\s+(myself|yourself|himself|herself|themselves|ourselves)\b/i or sentence-initial /\b(myself|yourself)\s+(and|will|am|have|had)\b/i

incorrect: Myself and John went to the meeting.
correct: John and I went to the meeting.

exceptions: Correct: 'I hurt myself.' (subject = object). 'I myself did it.' (emphasis).
rules.fix_hint: Replace the reflexive pronoun with 'me', 'I', etc. as appropriate.

rules.title: Uncountable noun used with a/an or pluralized
rules.description: Uncountable nouns cannot take the indefinite article a/an and cannot be pluralized.
rules.pattern_description: Match (a|an) + uncountable noun or uncountable noun + plural -s. Uncountable nouns: information, advice, furniture, equipment, luggage, baggage, news, progress, research, work, homework, evidence, knowledge, traffic, weather, money, music, art, luck, fun, happiness, sadness, anger, water, rice, bread, sugar, milk, coffee, tea, gold, silver, oxygen, electricity, software, hardware, feedback, data (in formal usage), clothing, scenery, poetry, machinery, vocabulary.

correct: He has a lot of luggage.

exceptions: Some nouns can be both: 'a coffee' (= a cup of coffee), 'works' (literary/artistic works), 'waters' (bodies of water).
rules.fix_hint: Remove the article or use a quantifier (some, a piece of, much). Remove plural -s.

rules.title: Irregular plural errors
rules.description: Some nouns have irregular plural forms that don't follow the -s/-es pattern.
rules.pattern_description: Match wrong plurals: childs→children, mans→men, womans→women, tooths→teeth, foots→feet, mouses→mice, gooses→geese, oxes→oxen, persons→people (in general sense), criterions→criteria, phenomenons→phenomena, datums→data, alumnus→alumni, cactuses→cacti (both accepted), funguses→fungi, syllabuses→syllabi, appendixs→appendices.
exceptions: 'Persons' is valid in legal/formal contexts. 'Cactuses' and 'cacti' both accepted.
rules.fix_hint: Replace the incorrect plural with the correct irregular form.

rules.title: Article a/an based on sound
rules.description: Use 'a' before consonant sounds and 'an' before vowel sounds — based on pronunciation, not spelling.
rules.pattern_description: Check the next word's initial sound. Words starting with silent h (hour, honest, honor, heir, herb) need 'an'. Words with consonant-sounding vowels (university, uniform, united, unique, European, one, once, useful) need 'a'. Acronyms depend on pronunciation (an FBI, a NASA, an HTML, a URL).
exceptions: Historical or regional pronunciation may vary: 'a/an historic' (both accepted).
rules.fix_hint: Switch 'a' to 'an' or vice versa based on the following word's SOUND.
rules.british_variant: 'An hotel' and 'an historic' are sometimes used in formal British English.

rules.title: Unnecessary 'the' with general/abstract nouns
rules.description: Do not use 'the' with uncountable or plural nouns when speaking in general terms.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\bthe\s+(life|love|death|nature|society|education|music|art|science|politics|history|time|happiness|freedom|democracy|justice|beauty|truth|knowledge|information|water|air)\s+(is|are|was|were|has|can|will|should|must)\b/i — when used in general philosophical sense.
exceptions: 'The life of the party' (specific). 'The music was too loud' (specific instance). 'The education system' (specific system).
rules.fix_hint: Remove 'the' when referring to the concept in general.

rules.title: Unnecessary 'the' with sports and meals
rules.description: Do not use 'the' before sports or meal names in general sense.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(play|plays|played|playing)\s+the\s+(tennis|football|soccer|basketball|baseball|cricket|golf|volleyball|badminton|hockey|rugby|chess)\b/i and /\bhad\s+the\s+(breakfast|lunch|dinner|supper|brunch)\b/i

incorrect: We had the dinner at home.
I had the lunch at noon.

exceptions: 'Play the piano/guitar' — musical instruments DO take 'the'. 'The dinner was excellent' (specific dinner).
rules.fix_hint: Remove 'the' before sport or meal names in general usage.

rules.title: Less with countable nouns
rules.description: Use 'fewer' with countable plural nouns. 'Less' is for uncountable nouns.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\bless\s+(items|things|people|words|books|cars|houses|dogs|cats|students|teachers|errors|problems|questions|answers|options|choices|opportunities|minutes|hours|days|weeks|months|years|dollars|euros|points|runs|goals|votes|members|employees|customers|passengers|participants|applicants|candidates|complaints|accidents|mistakes|changes|differences|similarities|examples|instances|cases|reasons|factors|elements|features|aspects|details|steps|stages|levels|layers|pieces|parts|sections|chapters|pages|paragraphs|sentences|lines|rows|columns|files|folders|tabs|windows|buttons|clicks|downloads|uploads|messages|emails|calls|meetings|events|tasks|projects|assignments|lessons|classes|courses|subjects|topics|ideas|concepts|theories|models|versions|updates|releases|issues|bugs|tickets|stories|articles|posts|comments|reviews|ratings|likes|shares|followers|subscribers|users|accounts|transactions|payments|orders|shipments|deliveries)\b/i
exceptions: 'Less than' with numbers/amounts is acceptable: 'less than 10 miles', 'less than $50'. Supermarket '10 items or less' is traditional.
rules.fix_hint: Replace 'less' with 'fewer' before countable plural nouns.

rules.title: Much with countable nouns or many with uncountable
rules.description: Use 'much' with uncountable nouns and 'many' with countable plural nouns.
rules.pattern_description: Match 'much' + countable plural noun or 'many' + uncountable noun.
exceptions: 'Much' in questions/negatives: 'Not much time.' is natural. 'How much time?' is correct.
rules.fix_hint: Replace 'much' with 'many' for countable nouns and vice versa.

rules.title: Amount with countable nouns
rules.description: Use 'number' with countable nouns and 'amount' with uncountable nouns.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(amount|amounts)\s+of\s+(people|items|things|students|books|cars|errors|problems|words|pages|files|changes|members|employees|customers|users|tasks|steps|questions|answers|ideas|options|choices|reasons|factors|examples|cases|details|features|attempts|complaints)\b/i

incorrect: The amount of people was large.
correct: The number of people was large.

exceptions: 'Amount of' is increasingly accepted with countable nouns in informal English.
rules.fix_hint: Replace 'amount of' with 'number of' before countable nouns.

rules.title: Enough before adjective instead of after
rules.description: 'Enough' goes AFTER adjectives and adverbs, but BEFORE nouns.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\benough\s+(big|tall|good|strong|fast|old|young|smart|rich|warm|cold|hot|cool|large|small|high|low|wide|deep|long|short|loud|quiet|hard|soft|bright|dark|clean|clear|safe|brave|kind|nice|sweet|easy|difficult|important|interesting|cheap|expensive)\b/i
exceptions: Before nouns: 'enough money', 'enough time', 'enough space' — this is correct.
rules.fix_hint: Move 'enough' to after the adjective/adverb.

rules.title: Adjective used instead of adverb
rules.description: Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs. Use the -ly form, not the adjective, after action verbs.
rules.pattern_description: Match action verb + adjective where adverb is needed: 'runs quick'→'runs quickly', 'speaks loud'→'speaks loudly', 'drives slow'→'drives slowly', 'writes bad'→'writes badly'.
exceptions: Flat adverbs: 'drive fast' (not 'fastly'), 'work hard' (not 'hardly', which means 'barely'). After linking verbs: 'She looks good' (adjective is correct).
rules.fix_hint: Add -ly to the adjective to form the correct adverb.
rules.british_variant: 'Slow/slowly' — both accepted as adverbs in British English.

rules.title: Double comparative
rules.description: Do not combine 'more' with the -er comparative form or 'most' with the -est superlative form.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\bmore\s+(bigger|smaller|taller|shorter|faster|slower|older|younger|easier|harder|simpler|wider|narrower|louder|quieter|nicer|safer|cleaner|closer|newer|darker|brighter|stronger|weaker|smarter|cheaper|richer|poorer|thinner|thicker|longer|lighter|heavier|cooler|warmer|hotter|colder|drier|wetter)\b/i and /\bmost\s+(biggest|smallest|tallest|shortest|fastest|slowest|oldest|youngest|easiest|hardest|simplest|widest|narrowest|loudest|quietest|nicest|safest|cleanest|closest|newest|darkest|brightest|strongest|weakest|smartest|cheapest|richest|poorest|thinnest|thickest|longest|lightest|heaviest|coolest|warmest|hottest|coldest|driest|wettest)\b/i
rules.fix_hint: Remove 'more' or 'most' — the -er/-est form already expresses comparison.

rules.title: Regularized irregular comparative/superlative
rules.description: Some adjectives have irregular comparative/superlative forms that must be used instead of adding -er/-est.
rules.pattern_description: Match: gooder→better, goodest→best, badder→worse, baddest→worst, more good→better, most good→best, more bad→worse, most bad→worst, more well→better, most well→best, littler→less, farer→farther/further.

incorrect: She is more good at math.
This is the most good solution.

exceptions: 'Baddest' is slang for 'toughest/coolest'. 'Littler' is sometimes used informally for 'smaller'.
rules.fix_hint: Replace with the correct irregular form.
rules.british_variant: farther (distance) vs further (degree/metaphorical) — British uses 'further' for both.

rules.title: Comparing absolute adjectives
rules.description: Absolute adjectives (unique, perfect, complete, dead, infinite, impossible, universal, absolute, essential, fatal, final, total, eternal, entire, supreme) cannot logically be compared.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(more|most|very|extremely|really|somewhat|rather|slightly|fairly|absolutely)\s+(unique|perfect|complete|dead|infinite|impossible|universal|absolute|essential|fatal|final|total|eternal|entire|supreme|unanimous|empty|full|equal|round|square|true|false|pregnant|correct|incorrect)\b/i
exceptions: 'A more perfect union' — from US Constitution, accepted as established phrase. 'Very' can sometimes mean 'truly/in every sense': 'the very essence'.
rules.fix_hint: Remove the modifier or use 'nearly', 'almost', or 'virtually' instead.

rules.title: -ed vs -ing adjective confusion
rules.description: -ed adjectives describe how someone FEELS; -ing adjectives describe what CAUSES the feeling.
rules.pattern_description: Match person/pronoun + 'is/am/are/was/were/feel/felt' + wrong form: 'I am boring' (should be bored), 'The movie is bored' (should be boring).

incorrect: We are exciting about the trip.
correct: We are excited about the trip.

exceptions: 'She is boring' can be correct if describing her personality (she causes boredom). Context is key.
rules.fix_hint: Use -ed when describing a person's feelings, -ing when describing the cause.

rules.title: Good vs well confusion
rules.description: 'Good' is an adjective (modifies nouns). 'Well' is an adverb (modifies verbs). After linking verbs, use 'good' for quality and 'well' for health.
rules.pattern_description: Match action verbs + 'good' where 'well' is needed: 'plays good', 'writes good', 'performs good', 'works good', 'functions good', 'runs good', 'speaks good', 'sings good', 'dances good', 'cooks good'.
exceptions: After linking verbs: 'I feel good' (emotional state — correct). 'I feel well' (health — also correct). 'She looks good' (appearance — correct).
rules.fix_hint: Replace 'good' with 'well' after action verbs.

rules.title: Wrong preposition after common verbs
rules.description: Many verbs require specific prepositions. Using the wrong preposition is a common error.
rules.pattern_description: Match: 'discuss about'→'discuss', 'reach to'→'reach', 'return back'→'return', 'enter into' (room)→'enter', 'cope up with'→'cope with', 'comprise of'→'comprise'/'is composed of', 'emphasize on'→'emphasize', 'mention about'→'mention', 'explain me'→'explain to me', 'request for'→'request', 'demand for'→'demand', 'order for'→'order', 'approach to'→'approach'.

incorrect: I can't cope up with it.
The team comprises of five members.

exceptions: 'enter into' is correct for agreements: 'enter into a contract'. 'return to' (destination) is correct.
rules.fix_hint: Remove the unnecessary preposition or replace with the correct one.

rules.title: Wrong time preposition (at/on/in)
rules.description: Use 'at' for specific times, 'on' for days/dates, 'in' for months/years/seasons.
rules.pattern_description: Match: 'in Monday'→'on Monday', 'at January'→'in January', 'on 5 o'clock'→'at 5 o'clock', 'in Monday morning'→'on Monday morning', 'at 2024'→'in 2024'.
exceptions: 'At Christmas/Easter' (the holiday period) vs 'on Christmas Day' (the specific day). 'At night' (not 'in the night' in AmE). 'In the morning/afternoon/evening' but 'at night'.
rules.fix_hint: Replace with at (specific times/points), on (days/dates), in (months/years/periods).

rules.title: Wrong place preposition (at/in/on)
rules.description: Use 'at' for points/addresses, 'in' for enclosed spaces/areas, 'on' for surfaces.
rules.pattern_description: Match common errors: 'in the bus stop'→'at the bus stop', 'at the room'→'in the room', 'in the table'→'on the table', 'in a chair' (usually 'on').

incorrect: I'll meet you in the bus stop.
The book is in the table.
correct: I'll meet you at the bus stop.
The book is on the table.

exceptions: 'In the car' but 'on the bus/train/plane'. 'At home/work/school' (no 'the').
rules.fix_hint: Use 'at' for specific locations/points, 'in' for enclosed areas, 'on' for surfaces.

rules.title: Preposition followed by infinitive instead of gerund
rules.description: After prepositions, always use the gerund (-ing form), never the infinitive (to + verb).
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(interested in|good at|afraid of|tired of|capable of|fond of|instead of|in spite of|look forward to|accustomed to|used to|object to|committed to|dedicated to|devoted to|in addition to|prior to|with regard to|insist on|focus on|depend on|rely on|concentrate on|apologize for|responsible for|famous for|known for|apply for|accuse of|approve of|consist of|dream of|think of|talk about|worry about|care about|complain about|forget about|argue about|succeed in|believe in|participate in|result in|specialize in)\s+to\s+([a-z]+)\b/i — where the 'to' leads to a bare infinitive instead of gerund.

incorrect: I look forward to meet you.
She is interested in to learn.
I am used to to wake early.
correct: I look forward to meeting you.
I am used to waking early.

exceptions: 'Used to + infinitive' (past habit) is different from 'be used to + gerund' (accustomed to).
rules.fix_hint: Replace the infinitive with the -ing form after prepositions.

rules.title: Redundant preposition with verbs
rules.description: Some verbs already contain the directional meaning and do not need an additional preposition.
rules.pattern_description: Regex: /\b(return)\s+back\b|\b(repeat)\s+again\b|\b(revert)\s+back\b|\b(continue)\s+on\b|\b(combine)\s+together\b|\b(connect)\s+together\b|\b(join)\s+together\b|\b(merge)\s+together\b|\b(mix)\s+together\b|\b(penetrate)\s+into\b|\b(rise)\s+up\b|\b(descend)\s+down\b|\b(advance)\s+forward\b|\b(circle)\s+around\b|\b(lift)\s+up\b|\b(raise)\s+up\b/i

incorrect: Let's revert back to the old version.
correct: Let's revert to the old version.

exceptions: 'Repeat again' can be valid when emphasizing doing it a second time. Some are idiomatic and widely accepted.
rules.fix_hint: Remove the redundant preposition/adverb.

rules.title: In vs Into confusion
rules.description: 'In' indicates location (being inside). 'Into' indicates movement (going inside).
rules.pattern_description: Match movement verbs (walked, ran, jumped, dove, climbed, went, came, fell, stepped, rushed, stormed, burst, threw) + 'in the' where 'into the' is correct.

incorrect: The cat climbed in the box.
correct: The cat climbed into the box.

exceptions: 'Walk in the park' (location, not entering). Context determines meaning.
rules.fix_hint: Use 'into' with movement verbs when indicating entering a space.

NP.tokenization: Pronoun case and reflexive checks are regex-friendly. Antecedent agreement needs NLP coreference resolution for full accuracy.
NP.nlp_requirements: Use compromise for POS tagging to identify subjects vs objects. Countability requires a lexicon map of uncountable nouns.
NP.regex_applicability: NP_CAS_001-002, NP_WHO_001, NP_REF_001, NP_CNT_001, NP_PLU_001 are well-suited for regex.
NP.performance: Build an uncountable nouns Set for O(1) lookup.

AD.tokenization: Article checks require examining the phonetic onset of the following word — maintain a map of exception words.
AD.nlp_requirements: Fewer/less and much/many require determining noun countability — use a countable/uncountable lexicon.
AD.regex_applicability: All AD rules are regex-based. AD_AAN_001 needs a custom check function for phonetic onset.
AD.performance: Phonetic onset check: maintain Sets for consonant-sounding vowels (uni-, use-, eur-, one) and silent-h words (hour, honest, honor, heir, herb).

AA.tokenization: Adjective/adverb distinction requires POS tagging. Participial adjective checks need subject detection.
AA.nlp_requirements: Use compromise to tag #Adjective vs #Adverb positions. The -ed/-ing check benefits from subject animacy detection.
AA.regex_applicability: AA_CMP_001, AA_IRR_001, AA_ABS_001, AA_GDW_001 are strongly regex-based. AA_ADV_001 and AA_PAR_001 benefit from NLP.
AA.performance: Build Sets for absolute adjectives and irregular comparatives.

PR.tokenization: Preposition collocations are highly regex-friendly — maintain a Map<wrongCollocation, correctCollocation>.
PR.nlp_requirements: Place preposition checks may benefit from NLP to detect noun type (location vs surface). Time prepositions need date/time entity recognition.
PR.regex_applicability: All PR rules are well-suited for regex with word-boundary matching.
PR.performance: Collocation checks should use a single combined regex with alternation for efficiency.
